title: Memory Management & GC
description: Python memory management from reference counting to Staff+ GC tuning: refcounts, cyclic GC, weakrefs, memory profiling, and leak detection.

sections.title: 1. Reference Counting — The Primary GC
sections.description:
Every Python object has a refcount (accessible via sys.getrefcount() which itself increments it). When refcount hits 0, the object is immediately deallocated. This is deterministic and synchronous.

Incrementing refcount: assignment (a = b), passing as argument, adding to container. Decrementing: del, reassignment, exiting scope, container deletion.

FAANG angle: 'Why does Python have both reference counting and a cyclic GC?' — reference counting handles most cases immediately, but cannot handle circular references (A references B, B references A). The cyclic GC handles these by detecting and collecting unreachable cycles.

ponytail: Not covering __del__ finalization pitfalls here — the short version is: never use __del__; use contextlib instead.
sections.codeBlock:
import sys
import gc

def show_refcount(obj, label=''):
    # sys.getrefcount adds 1 (temporary reference from the call)
    rc = sys.getrefcount(obj) - 1
    print(f'{label} refcount: {rc}')

class Node:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.ref = None

# Reference counting in action
a = Node('a')
show_refcount(a, 'after a = Node()')  # 1

b = a
show_refcount(a, 'after b = a')       # 2

lst = [a]
show_refcount(a, 'after lst = [a]')   # 3

del b
show_refcount(a, 'after del b')      # 2

del lst
show_refcount(a, 'after del lst')    # 1

del a  # refcount hits 0 — deallocated immediately

# Circular reference — cyclic GC needed
x = Node('x')
y = Node('y')
x.ref = y
y.ref = x
print(f'gc.garbage before del: {len(gc.garbage)}')
del x
del y
print(f'gc.garbage after del: {len(gc.garbage)}')  # 0 (collected by cyclic GC)

sections.title: 2. Cyclic Garbage Collector — Generational GC
sections.description:
Python's cyclic GC handles objects that reference counting misses. It's in gc module and uses three generations. Objects that survive collection get promoted to older generations, which are collected less frequently.

Thresholds (gc.get_threshold()): default is (700, 10, 10) — gen0 collected after 700 allocations minus deallocations, gen1 after 10 gen0 collections, gen2 after 10 gen1 collections.

gc.set_debug(gc.DEBUG_LEAK) prints unreachable objects for debugging.

FAANG angle: 'How do you debug a memory leak in Python?' — (1) gc.set_debug(gc.DEBUG_LEAK) shows unreachable objects. (2) objgraph.show_growth() tracks which types are growing. (3) tracemalloc for tracing allocations to source lines.
sections.codeBlock:
import gc

# Check GC settings
print(f'thresholds: {gc.get_threshold()}')  # (700, 10, 10)
print(f'generations: {gc.get_count()}')      # (live obj count per gen)

# Manual collection
gc.collect()       # full collection
# gc.collect(0)    # only gen0
# gc.collect(1)    # only gen1 + gen0
# gc.collect(2)    # full (all generations)

# Disable GC temporarily (rarely useful)
# gc.disable()

# Debug memory leaks
gc.set_debug(gc.DEBUG_LEAK)

class Leaky:
    def __init__(self):
        self.circular = self  # self-reference cycle

x = Leaky()
del x
print('unreachable objects:', gc.collect())
gc.set_debug(0)  # reset debug flags

# tracemalloc for line-by-line allocation tracing
import tracemalloc

tracemalloc.start()
d = {i: str(i) * 100 for i in range(1000)}
snapshot = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
for stat in snapshot.statistics('lineno')[:3]:
    print(stat)

sections.title: 3. Weak References — Break Cycles, Cache Smarter
sections.description:
weakref.ref holds a reference that doesn't increment the refcount. If the object is otherwise deallocated, the weakref returns None. Use WeakValueDictionary for caches and WeakKeyDictionary for metadata attached to objects.

Common uses: caches (don't prevent eviction), observers (don't keep dead listeners alive), tree structures (parent pointers without cycles).

FAANG angle: 'Design a cache that doesn't prevent garbage collection' — WeakValueDictionary. The cache holds weak references; when the object is no longer used elsewhere, it's evicted automatically.
sections.codeBlock:
import weakref
import gc

class ExpensiveModel:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        print(f'creating {name}')
    def __repr__(self):
        return f'Model({self.name})'

# Weak reference
model = ExpensiveModel('bert-base')
ref = weakref.ref(model)
print(ref())  # Model(bert-base)

del model
gc.collect()
print(ref())  # None (object deallocated)

# WeakValueDictionary — auto-evicting cache
cache = weakref.WeakValueDictionary()

class Data:
    def __init__(self, value):
        self.value = value

d1 = Data('expensive_result')
cache['key1'] = d1
print(cache['key1'].value)  # expensive_result

del d1
print(cache.get('key1'))    # None (auto-evicted)

# WeakKeyDictionary — metadata on objects
meta = weakref.WeakKeyDictionary()

class Widget:
    pass

w = Widget()
meta[w] = {'created_at': '2026-01-01'}
print(meta[w])  # {'created_at': '2026-01-01'}

del w
# meta entry garbage collected automatically

sections.title: 4. Memory Profiling — Finding Leaks and Waste
sections.description:
tracemalloc (stdlib, Python 3.4+): tracks memory allocations per file/line. Use it to trace where memory goes in production workloads.

objgraph: visualizes object graphs. objgraph.show_growth() shows which types are accumulating. objgraph.show_refs() produces a DOT graph of reachable objects (identifies leak roots).

pympler: pympler.asizeof.asizeof() gives accurate object sizes. muppy and class_tracker monitor class-level memory usage.

FAANG angle: 'Your Python service memory usage grows over 24 hours. How do you debug it?' — (1) tracemalloc to find what's allocating. (2) objgraph.show_growth() to see accumulating types. (3) gc.get_objects() to inspect live objects. (4) Check weakrefs and circular references.
sections.codeBlock:
# === tracemalloc (stdlib) ===
import tracemalloc

def profile_allocation():
    tracemalloc.start()

    # Simulate memory-heavy operation
    big = [bytearray(1000) for _ in range(10000)]

    snapshot = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
    stats = snapshot.statistics('lineno')

    for stat in stats[:3]:
        print(f'{stat.count:>6} x {stat.size:>10} B - {stat.traceback.format()[0]}')

    tracemalloc.stop()

# === objgraph ===
import objgraph

class GrowingList:
    def __init__(self):
        self.items = []

leaks = [GrowingList() for _ in range(100)]
objgraph.show_growth(limit=5)  # prints: GrowingList 100 (+100)

# === pympler ===
from pympler import asizeof

print(asizeof.asizeof({'a': [1, 2, 3]}))  # accurate size in bytes

# === sys.getsizeof (rough, doesn't recurse into containers) ===
import sys
print(sys.getsizeof([1, 2, 3]))  # 96 B (list overhead + pointer array)
# ↑ does NOT include the int objects (28 B each)

sections.title: 5. Staff+ Patterns — Container Optimization and GC Tuning
sections.description:
__slots__ replaces __dict__ with a statically-sized array. Saves ~40-80 bytes per instance. Use for dataclasses with millions of instances. array.array stores C-native types in a contiguous buffer. __pypy__ uses a different GC (generational, not refcount-based) — __del__ and weakref behavior differs.

GC tuning: Increase thresholds for batch jobs to avoid GC pauses. gc.set_threshold(100000, 100, 100) reduces collection frequency at the cost of larger pauses. gc.freeze() prevents the GC from scanning immutable objects (useful after loading a model).

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design a memory-efficient cache for billions of embeddings' — use __slots__ data classes, array('f') for float storage, WeakValueDictionary for cache eviction, and gc.freeze() after loading the embedding table.
sections.codeBlock:
import gc
import array

# === __slots__ benchmark ===
class Point:
    __slots__ = ('x', 'y')
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x, self.y = x, y

class PointDict:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x, self.y = x, y

import sys
print('slots size:', sys.getsizeof(Point(1, 2)))      # 56 B
print('dict size:', sys.getsizeof(PointDict(1, 2)))   # 72 B

# === array.array for numeric data ===
nums = array.array('d', [1.0, 2.0, 3.0])  # 'd' = double (8 B each)
# list of floats would store PyObject pointers (8 B) + float objects (24 B each)

# === gc.freeze for immutable data ===
# After loading a large model, freeze to skip future scans:
gc.freeze()  # prevents GC from scanning immutable objects

# === Manual collection for batch jobs ===
for batch in range(10):
    process_batch(batch)
    if batch % 5 == 0:
        collected = gc.collect()
        print(f'collected {collected} objects after batch {batch}')

# === gc.disable for latency-sensitive services ===
# gc.disable() + periodic gc.collect() at known safe points

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• gc module docs
  • weakref module
  • tracemalloc module
  • objgraph — object graph visualization
  • CPython Internals by Anthony Shaw — GC implementation chapter

details:
Memory management workflow: (1) Understand refcounting — it handles 95% of objects. (2) Use weakrefs for caches, observers, and parent pointers. (3) Profile with tracemalloc before optimizing. (4) Use __slots__ for high-volume data classes. (5) Tune GC thresholds for batch vs real-time workloads.

Common mistakes: Circular references in __del__ methods (prevents GC); using weakrefs when ownership is clear; not disabling GC during model serving (causes latency spikes); relying on sys.getsizeof for accurate container sizes (doesn't recurse).

Staff+ expectations: Design memory-efficient data structures for high-scale services. Understand GC behavior well enough to debug leaks and tune throughput. Know when to reach for array.array, __slots__, and gc.freeze.
